source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure no 'root' user account access key exists

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure no 'root' user account access key exists
input_row.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. AWS Access Keys provide programmatic access to a given AWS account. It is recommended that all access keys associated with the 'root' user account be deleted.
input_row.rationale: Deleting access keys associated with the 'root' user account limits vectors by which the account can be compromised. Additionally, deleting the 'root' access keys encourages the creation and use of role based accounts that are least privileged.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if the 'root' user account has access keys: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console. 2. Click Services. 3. Click IAM. 4. Click on Credential Report. 5. This will download a .csv file which contains credential usage for all IAM users within an AWS Account - open this file. 6. For the <root_account> user, ensure the access_key_1_active and access_key_2_active fields are set to FALSE. From Command Line: Run the following command: aws iam get-account-summary | grep "AccountAccessKeysPresent" If no 'root' access keys exist the output will show "AccountAccessKeysPresent": 0,. If the output shows a "1", then 'root' keys exist and should be deleted.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to delete active 'root' user access keys. From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console as 'root' and open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. Click on <root_account> at the top right and select My Security Credentials from the drop down list. 3. On the pop out screen Click on Continue to Security Credentials. 4. Click on Access Keys (Access Key ID and Secret Access Key). 5. If there are active keys, under Status, click Delete (Note: Deleted keys cannot be recovered). Note: While a key can be made inactive, this inactive key will still show up in the CLI command from the audit procedure, and may lead to the root user being falsely flagged as being non-compliant. References: 1. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws-access-keys-best- practices.html 2. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/managing-aws-access-keys.html 3. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/APIReference/API_GetAccountSummary .html 4. CCE-78910-7 5. https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/an-easier-way-to-determine-the- presence-of-aws-account-access-keys/ Additional Information: • IAM User account "root" for us-gov cloud regions is not enabled by default. However, on request to AWS support enables 'root' access only through access- keys (CLI, API methods) for us-gov cloud region. • Implement regular checks and alerts for any creation of new root access keys to promptly address any unauthorized or accidental creation.
input_row.references: 1. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws-access-keys-best- practices.html 2. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/managing-aws-access-keys.html 3. http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/APIReference/API_GetAccountSummary .html 4. CCE-78910-7 5. https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/an-easier-way-to-determine-the- presence-of-aws-account-access-keys/ Additional Information: • IAM User account "root" for us-gov cloud regions is not enabled by default. However, on request to AWS support enables 'root' access only through access- keys (CLI, API methods) for us-gov cloud region. • Implement regular checks and alerts for any creation of new root access keys to promptly address any unauthorized or accidental creation.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control "Ensure no 'root' user account access key exists" can be thoroughly verified using AWS APIs or the AWS CLI. The audit description provides a command that utilizes the AWS CLI to check for the presence of root account access keys, which indicates the feasibility of an automated approach. Additionally, IAM and credential management are standard AWS service properties that are accessible through APIs and the AWS CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_root_user_access_keys_absence

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the boto3 `iam` client to access IAM functionalities.
- Specifically, utilize the `get_account_summary()` method to determine if any root user access keys exist.
- This method provides an overview of key IAM account details under the key `"AccountAccessKeysPresent"`, which is useful to confirm the presence of access keys.
- Implement the following logic:
  1. Initialize a boto3 session with appropriate IAM permissions.
  2. Call the `get_account_summary()` method.
  3. Inspect the returned summary for the `"AccountAccessKeysPresent"` attribute.
  4. Validate the attribute value; ensure it reports `0` (zero) indicating compliance.
- Pass/Fail Criteria:
  - **Pass:** The value of `"AccountAccessKeysPresent"` is `0`, confirming no root access keys exist.
  - **Fail:** The value is `1`, indicating the presence of root access keys.
